title: transaction — subprocessors
description: The Transaction module engages up to THREE third-party subprocessors — all for exchange-rate lookup during cross-currency transactions. Tenants using multi-currency (Medium+ tier) can choose one at settings-time; tenants operating single-currency engage NO subprocessors. All three are FX-rate data providers; no PII / no financial account data / no card data flows through any of them (they receive only currency-pair codes like 'USD-EUR' at rate-fetch time). GDPR Art. 28 DPA references + Art. 30 records of processing activities apply.

subprocessors.purpose: Exchange rate lookup for cross-currency transactions. Rate is captured at commit + frozen on the transaction row (exchange_rate_to_base + base_amount_cents). Provides 200+ currency pairs updated hourly.
subprocessors.data_hashing: N/A — no PII flows through this provider
subprocessors.activation_gate: tenant admin selects this provider in transaction settings + acknowledges DPA reference

subprocessors.purpose: Exchange rate lookup for cross-currency transactions. Alternative to Open Exchange Rates. Provides 170+ currencies. Real-time + historical data.
subprocessors.activation_gate: tenant admin selects this provider + acknowledges DPA

subprocessors.purpose: Official ECB daily reference rates. Fallback provider when the primary provider is unreachable. Free + no auth required. Government-sourced — regulator-preferred rate source for EU jurisdictions.
subprocessors.dpa_url: N/A — public government data; no DPA required
subprocessors.activation_gate: always available as fallback — no tenant activation needed

downstream_subprocessors_referenced.used_for: Dispatching LedgerImbalanceDetectedNotification / TransactionAmendmentAppliedNotification / AccountBalanceAlertNotification / MonthlyLedgerReportNotification via mail / SMS / push channels.

Archiving transactions + ledger entries to compliance-storage before hard-purge. The archive backend is S3 with Object Lock (AWS Compliance region) — AWS is the subprocessor. Also used by TenantErased archive flow.
Every write to transactions / transaction_ledger_entries writes an audit row via the audit module. The audit module's storage subprocessor (typically AWS RDS / DocumentDB) is engaged transitively.

notes: This module engages EXACTLY THREE subprocessors — all FX-rate providers. Contrast with growth::marketing which engages 9 (one per ad network) — the transaction module's external surface is much smaller because no PII flows out.

The transaction module DOES NOT engage payment gateway subprocessors (Stripe / Paddle / etc.) — those are the payment module's concern. This module records the MOVEMENT of money that happened via those gateways, but never talks to them directly.

The transaction module DOES NOT engage bank / clearing / ACH subprocessors — those are the bank_deposits + bank_withdrawals record entries fed by the payment module. The transaction module records the movement; the payment module talks to the banking infrastructure.

ECB Daily is not really a 'subprocessor' in the GDPR sense (no data flows; public data pull) — included for completeness because it's referenced in the compliance narrative + regulators may ask 'where does the ECB rate come from'.
